g.data-narration: Welcome to Top-Down Parsing - a fundamental technique in compiler design for analyzing input strings.
g.text: Building parse trees from start symbol to terminals

g.data-narration: Top-down parsing builds the parse tree from the root (start symbol) downward, matching input symbols at each step.
g.text: • Constructs parse tree from root (start symbol) to leaves (terminals)
• Begins with start symbol, applies production rules to match input
• Intuitive method forming basis for predictive parsing in compilers

g.data-narration: There are two main approaches: Recursive Descent uses procedures for each non-terminal, while LL(1) uses predictive tables.
Left recursion occurs when a production starts with the same non-terminal, causing infinite recursion in parsers.
g.text: A production of form A → A causes infinite recursion
"E expands to E+T, which expands to E+T+T, and so on... infinite loop!"

g.data-narration: To eliminate left recursion, we transform A → A| into A → and A' → A'| using a new non-terminal.
g.text: E → T E' and E' → +T E' | ε

g.data-narration: Left factoring removes common prefixes from productions by introducing a new non-terminal to factor out shared parts.
g.text: When two productions share a common prefix, factor them out
Original: stmt → if expr then stmt else stmt
stmt → if expr then stmt rest

g.data-narration: FIRST() contains all terminals that can appear as the first symbol of strings derived from - essential for predictive parsing.
g.text: FIRST() = { terminals that can be first symbol of strings derived from }
Rules: 1) X terminal → {X} 2) X→ε → add ε 3) X→YY... → include FIRST(Y)-{}, then FIRST(Y) if ε∈FIRST(Y), and so on

g.data-narration: Let's compute FIRST sets for grammar E→TE', E'→+TE'|ε, T→FT', T'→*FT'|ε, F→(E)|id.
FOLLOW(A) is the set of terminals that can appear immediately after non-terminal A in any sentential form.
g.text: FOLLOW(A) = { terminals that can appear immediately after A in sentential forms }
Rules: 1) Add $ to FOLLOW(S) 2) A→B → add FIRST()-{ε} to FOLLOW(B) 3) A→B or A→B with ε∈FIRST() → add FOLLOW(A) to FOLLOW(B)

g.data-narration: Using the same grammar, we compute FOLLOW sets by applying rules - start symbol gets $ in FOLLOW.
A grammar is LL(1) if: no ambiguity, no left recursion, and alternatives have disjoint FIRST sets.
g.text: A grammar is LL(1) if ALL conditions hold:

g.data-narration: To build LL(1) parsing table: for each production A, add to M[A,a] for terminals a in FIRST(), and if in FIRST(), add to M[A,b] for b in FOLLOW(A).
g.text: 1) For each a ∈ FIRST(): add A→ to M[A,a]
2) If ε ∈ FIRST(): for b ∈ FOLLOW(A), add to M[A,b]
No multiple entries → Grammar is LL(1)

g.data-narration: The predictive parser uses a stack: pop terminals to match input, pop non-terminals and push production RHS reversed using the parsing table.
g.text: • If X = terminal: match or error

g.data-narration: Here's the complete LL(1) parsing table for our example grammar - each cell shows which production to apply.
Let's trace parsing for input id+id*id: watch how the stack changes and productions are applied step by step.

g.text: Step 1: $E → TE' → FT' → id (match) → ε → +TE' → ε → accept

g.data-narration: Key formulas to remember: left recursion elimination, left factoring transformation, FIRST and FOLLOW computation rules.
Remember: LL(1) means Left-to-right scan, Leftmost derivation, One symbol lookahead. Always eliminate left recursion first!
g.text: • LL(1): L=Left-to-right, L=Leftmost derivation, 1=One symbol lookahead
• Always remove LEFT RECURSION first before building parsing table
• Compute FIRST sets before FOLLOW sets • Check LL(1) conditions before table construction
